data.title: FELC: Alternative Exposure To The S&P 500, Positive Track Record
data.description:
Fidelity Enhanced Large Cap Core ETF (NYSEARCA:FELC) leans on fundamentals and market leadership at the same time, tilting toward mega-cap growth stocks while avoiding excessive valuations of today’s market. This is a fund for investors looking to benefit from its growth potential, particularly if corporate earnings continue to support big tech and the stock market in general.

ETF Description & Highlights

FELC invests primarily in companies listed in the S&P 500 index. It seeks diversified exposure to stocks that, based on its research, exhibit fundamentals, including valuation, growth, and quality characteristics that are expected to translate into price outperformance over time.

As of August 18, 2025, FELC had $4.9 million in assets invested in 189 holdings, with an average market cap of $402.86 billion. The fund has a heavy allocation to mega caps, which account for 47.8% of total assets, slightly above the 47.1% in the S&P 500, represented here by the SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY). With large caps making up 31.5% and mid caps 15.7%, FELC’s exposure across market caps closely mirrors the S&P 500.

FELC’s top ten holdings are heavily skewed toward technology. Its largest positions are NVIDIA (8.38%), Apple (6.76%), and Microsoft (6.43%), which together account for over one-fifth of the portfolio. The sector exposure also includes Broadcom (3.13%), reinforcing the fund’s strong technology bias. Outside of the technology sector, Amazon (4.33%), Meta Platforms (3.39%), and Alphabet’s two share classes (2.28% and 2.14%) give the fund substantial weight in consumer discretionary and communication services. Completing FELC’s largest ten positions, shares of Berkshire Hathaway (2.07%) and JPMorgan Chase (1.78%) represent the financial services sector.

Once FELC blends different fundamental criteria in its investment process, such as valuation, growth, and quality, I consider it suitable to pair FELC with ETFs that also employ multi-factor and fundamentals-based strategies. These funds are listed below.

The first is the iShares U.S. Equity Factor ETF (LRGF), which screens U.S. stocks using a rules-based methodology focused on five factors (value, quality, momentum, size, and low volatility). Next, the SPDR® MSCI USA StrategicFactors ETF (QUS) follows a similar approach, tracking an MSCI index that blends value, quality, and low-volatility components equally. Franklin U.S. Large Cap Multifactor Index ETF (FLQL) has a quality, momentum, and value tilt instead, weighting constituents through a proprietary methodology. The John Hancock Multifactor Large Cap ETF (JHML) applies a multifactor model that focuses on profitability and value. Finally, Vident U.S. Equity Strategy ETF (VUSE) weights stocks not just by factor scores but also by corporate governance and accounting quality, while the Pacer Lunt Large Cap Multi-Factor Alternator ETF (PALC) adopts a monthly rotation process that selects the best-performing factor among four distinct measures (momentum, quality, volatility, and value).

FELC's largest allocation is to technology at 36.6%, followed by financial services (13.4%) and communication services (12.0%). The fund also holds meaningful positions in consumer discretionary (11.3%), healthcare (8.6%), and industrials (7.9%). Meanwhile, minor weights appear in consumer staples (3.5%), energy (2.7%), basic materials (2.5%), utilities (0.8%), and real estate (0.7%).

The fund’s allocation to technology is marked by core holdings in semiconductors such as NVIDIA, Broadcom, and Qualcomm, while the exposure to the software segment is concentrated in names like Microsoft, Salesforce, and Adobe. Beyond these two areas, Apple remains one of FELC’s top positions, not only in the sector but at the fund level as well.

In financial services, the fund has a broad-based allocation profile. Its largest holdings include global payment players Mastercard and Visa. The banking segment is also well represented through positions in JPMorgan and Bank of America. The exposure to the sector is complemented by holdings in investment banks and brokerage firms (e.g., Goldman Sachs and Charles Schwab) and insurers like Progressive and Travelers.

Outside these top sectors, FELC still has a representative allocation to communication services due to holdings in Meta Platforms, Alphabet, and Netflix, while Amazon and Tesla are the fund’s core holdings in consumer discretionary. Holdings in healthcare (AbbVie, Merck, and Gilead), energy (EOG Resources and Williams), and industrials (e.g., GE Aerospace and Trane Technologies) add further diversification to the fund.

FELC has the highest exposure to technology among peers, as funds like QUS, JHML, and PALC take a somewhat conservative approach to the sector, likely driven by a stronger bias toward value at this moment. On the other hand, FELC is underweight in defensive sectors like consumer staples (-3.4%), utilities (-1.7%), financial services (-1.5%), and healthcare (-1.4%). Although these are relatively narrow gaps, they highlight the fund’s overall more aggressive stance compared to peers.

Still, FELC has a high sector correlation with most peers, most of which are over 0.90, with the exception of PALC (0.81). The individual stock overlap is also moderate to high in the range of 0.40 to 0.56. However, FELC’s highest correlation is with the S&P 500 index at 0.62. This underscores the fund’s bias toward a neutral position rather than a meaningful tilt toward one of its core investment factors.

Valuation Slightly Below The S&P 500

FELC’s P/E of 21.3x represents a 9.2% discount to the S&P 500’s 23.5x. This reflects the fund’s cheaper valuations in sectors like technology, consumer discretionary, and healthcare, primarily due to underweight exposure to high-multiple companies, including Tesla and Palantir, alongside heavier positions in value plays in healthcare (e.g., Bristol Myers and Merck).

The fund has seen stronger earnings growth compared to the S&P 500 (12.4% vs. 9.2%), which is consistent with its modest underweight allocation to mature sectors like consumer staples, healthcare, and utilities. On profitability, FELC’s EBITDA margin of 34.9% and ROE of 48.2% are both in line with the S&P 500, with core sectors like technology and financials exhibiting a similar margin profile compared to the benchmark. This highlights the fund’s high sector correlation with the market cap indexes and a relatively neutral stock allocation.

FELC’s valuation is largely in the range of the peer group, with peers LRGF and JHML nearly matching FELC. But there are two outliers. VUSE (23.4x P/E) and PALC (24.6x P/E) show higher valuations, reflecting their stock selection in technology. This condition is exacerbated in PALC due to its high exposure to the consumer discretionary sector. Despite that, we can still see that, on average, FELC trades near parity with the peer group.

Outperforming Most Peers

FELC’s annualized returns have been in line with the S&P 500’s gains of 14.0%, with an outperformance during market stress, such as in 2022. On the other hand, FELC has done better than peers, particularly in the past two years, recording the second-best returns in the 5-year window, lagging behind only LRGF.

The fund’s volatility has been marginally lower than the S&P 500, with beta in the 0.97 - 0.98 range. While most peers have delivered similar volatility, funds like QUS and FLQL have shown lower beta and more contained price action than the broad market, both in up and down moves.

FELC is the most liquid fund in the group, with a daily volume of $29.9 million and an average bid-ask spread of 0.02%, the lowest among peers. Its turnover of 58% is higher than most funds, while the fund’s expense ratio (0.18%) is acceptable, though higher than LRGF, QUS, and FLQL. FELC’s TTM dividend yield of 1.01% is lower than the broad equity indexes (around 1.10%) and most peers as well, influenced by its light stance in above-average yielding sectors like healthcare, consumer staples, real estate, and utilities. Meanwhile, its tracking error of nearly 1% is quite low compared to most ETFs in the market. Finally, FELC’s Sharpe ratios of 0.79 (3-year) and 0.85 (5-year) place the fund at the top tier, along with LRGF and FLQL, thanks to strong returns and muted volatility.

Final Thoughts

With an investment strategy blending value, growth, and quality, FELC’s portfolio maintains a mega-cap tilt, similar to broad equity indexes. The combination of heavy positions in big techs and underweight in mature sectors like consumer staples and utilities underscores its above-average growth profile. Still, the fund trades at a slightly lower P/E compared to the S&P 500, driven by a stock selection that underweights some froth in the current stock market.

To be sure, mixed economic signals, amid uncertainties related to tariffs and rate cuts, pose a challenge for stocks to continue to grind higher, limiting the upside, at least in the short term. On the other hand, with strong corporate earnings supporting the market, I believe there is still a case for steady accumulation in this market. But selectivity is key, as always. That said, my view is that FELC’s reasonable valuation, positive track record, and historical contained volatility leave this fund among the good options for investors looking for exposure to growth in the long run.

data.title: Should You Invest $100,000 In Today's Market?
data.description:
Introduction:

The market has seen a lot of turmoil and volatility in 2025, but that already appears to be in the rear-view mirror. For now, in the last two months, the market sentiment has totally flipped to the positive. In fact, the S&P500 has been making new all-time highs, almost on a regular basis. So far, the gains have been impressive. This has been the result of many positive developments in the last two months. First, there was a thaw in the trade relationship between the two largest economies of the world, the U.S. and China. The negotiations are still ongoing, but everyone believes that neither country wants to go back to the tariff situation of April. Then there were a number of trade agreements that have been announced, the major ones being with the UK, the European Union, Japan, and South Korea. Further, the so-called "One Big, Beautiful Bill" was passed and signed into law. Even though it was hugely controversial, especially with its long-term impact on deficits, there were many positive aspects in the bill. First, no one wants to go back to the tax regime that existed prior to 2017, as that would amount to a huge tax increase in many aspects. Secondly, the bill had many business-friendly provisions, such as a 100% deduction in the first year for qualified investment expenses.

Also, we should not forget the earnings momentum that was evident in the second-quarter earnings releases. As of August 8th, nearly 90% of the S&P500 companies had released their earnings, and 81% of the companies had reported either a positive EPS or revenue surprise or both.

The only big hurdle that still remains in the path of the market's current momentum is how the Fed is going to act in the coming months. After the unexpected job report in July (and the downward revision of earlier months), most market participants expect a rate cut in the next Fed's FOMC meeting in September. However, it is difficult to read what the Fed has in mind. Will there be an increase in the inflation reading due to tariffs? Will the Fed act simply on the basis of inflation but not care about employment numbers? If the Fed disappoints the market in the September meeting with regard to a cut, but also how it projects the future cuts, the market could take a big hit.

Now, the next question would be whether it is a good time to invest. It is very difficult, if not impossible, to predict accurately when the market will hit the top (the same is true for identifying bottoms). Nothing has changed today. There will always be a wall of worries for the market that the market must climb constantly.

So, we believe that we should always be investing gradually. For example, if we had $100,000 to invest today, what would we do? It is never a good idea to keep a large chunk of money uninvested. Inflation may have come down compared to what it was in 2021-2022, but it never goes away. It takes away a bite every day from any amount that is not invested. For example, a dollar in the year 2000 is worth only about 52 cents today, just by factoring in the official rate of inflation. Sure, you should keep a pre-determined amount for emergency cash that should always be in cash-like investments. Any other capital should be invested. So, if we had $100,000 today, we would deploy it gradually over the next three to six months, though the pace may vary depending on the market's direction in the next few months. We are also assuming that our time horizon is long-term, at least 5-10 years. Also, it is important to consider a strategy that you have a high level of conviction in, because if you don't believe in the strategy, you would not be able to hold onto it during a tough period.

In this article, we will provide three very different and unique strategies, and we hope that most folks can find one or the other suitable for their needs.

Know Your Risk-Tolerance:

It is very crucial that you know your risk tolerance. If you invest $100,000 today and it drops down to $60,000 after a few months due to a broader market downturn or deep correction, will you hold onto your investments, or would you bail out, maybe just at the bottom? So, two things are important: high conviction on your investment strategy and your capacity to tolerate drawdowns. If you can tolerate, say, only about 20% drawdowns, then you should choose a strategy that will likely cap your losses to that level.

Start Investing Early:

A dollar saved (and invested) at the age of 30 is much more valuable than $2 saved and invested at the age of 50. The simple reason is the time factor and compounding. So, while it is very important to start saving (and investing) at an early age, we emphasize that it is never too late to start investing. Let's say you are already 50 and have not saved much. Though it is not an ideal situation, it is still better to start investing now than waiting for another 10 years.

Though it is important to strive to increase your earnings, it may be even more important how much you save out of those earnings. For example, a couple who earns $200,000 annually and saves only 5% is likely to have much less in retirement than someone who earns $150,000 but saves 20%.

Three Strategies:

Below, we will discuss three of our popular strategies and hope you will find one of them helpful for your needs and personal situation. You could also combine them (keeping them in three buckets), which should result in a stress-free investment portfolio.

Strategy 1: Fixed Income Strategy

Strategy 2: High-Income Strategy

Strategy 3: Hedging And Low Drawdown Strategy

Strategy 1: Fixed Income Strategy

This strategy is usually meant for a select group of folks who are either highly conservative (risk-averse) or older retirees, where income and preservation of capital matter much more than the growth of the capital. However, they still want some level of growth to meet inflation. Not all funds in this strategy are "fixed income" type, but the overall portfolio should provide very reliable income. With this portfolio, since 2018 and until July 2025, the worst return in any single year would have been -8%, compared to -18% from the S&P500 (note that one of the securities was changed due to lack of history).

To achieve the above goals, we will allocate nearly 25% of the funds to cash-like securities (CDs and money-market). We also allocate 40% of the funds to a set of two dividend-stock ETFs (20% each), which will provide inflation protection as well as some moderate income. The remaining 35% will be allocated equally to two funds providing a high level of income, i.e., BDCs (Business Development Companies) and a fund of funds (closed-end funds). They will also provide a wide diversification in terms of asset classes.

This is a highly risk-averse portfolio but would still provide a reasonably high income of nearly $69,000 on a capital base of $1 million. Since 2018, on an annualized basis, the growth of this portfolio (including the income) would have been 9.3%, not all that bad, considering 25% of the portfolio is in cash-like securities.

Investments:

We will have 25% of the portfolio invested in totally safe cash-like investments to provide a safety cushion to the portfolio. In addition, nearly 40% (20% each) is invested in two of the very popular, low-cost, and relatively safe dividend and income ETFs, Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) and JPMorgan Equity Premium Income (JEPI). These two positions are going to provide not only a high income but also the necessary growth to the broader portfolio.

We also have 35% of the portfolio invested in two income vehicles (BIDZ and CEFS). These two are going to provide high income, diversification, and exposure to the broad economy, but also add to the risk.

VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) is an ETF that invests in a portfolio of BDCs (Business Development Companies). BIZD is an ETF that holds about 30 individual BDCs, so the risk is fairly distributed. BIZD is a passive fund that allocates based on market-cap weighting. Some of the largest BDCs, such as Ares Capital (ARCC) and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), are its largest holdings.

Saba Closed-End Funds ETF (CEFS) is a "fund of funds" ETF, and as such, it is highly diversified. It invests in closed-end funds and currently holds close to 100 funds. It is managed by SABA Capital Group and is an actively managed fund. It has a rather short history of less than 10 years. It also pays a monthly distribution, which accounts for roughly an 8.4% yield on the current prices. The expense ratio is high with the fund's fees plus the expenses of its underlying fund holdings, but it is a cost for wide diversification and active management. This is pretty much in line with funds of this nature.

Strategy 2: High-Income (8% income) Strategy

This strategy is for folks who believe in earning a high income from their investments, whether they use it all or reinvest it back into the portfolio. Sure, the high income comes with some added risk, but if the portfolio is properly diversified into different asset classes and sectors, and selects the best possible fund in each sector, the risk is very manageable. In the long term, such a portfolio can possibly provide market-matching returns as well.

Obviously, if you are someone who is comfortable with a 3% to 4% income from their portfolios because you either have a large amount of capital or income coming from other sources, then this strategy may not be for you.

The portfolio presented below will aim to generate a minimum of 8% income.

The securities/funds are: (JEPQ), (AMLP), (JFNIX), (O), (ARCC), (MAIN), (PDI), (XFLT), (ADX), (RFI), (UTG), and (PEO).

Table 2: High-Income Portfolio:

The portfolio shown above is highly diversified, spread across many sectors and asset classes. The portfolio has a total of 12 funds (including REITs and BDCs) with equal allocation. The average yield at this time is very close to 9%.

Strategy 3: The Hedging (SWAN - Sleep Well At Night) Investing

We are going to present a ROTATION method for income investors, using FOUR-PLUS-ONE securities. The first four are the core investments, while the fifth one is the hedging security. This strategy can also provide a significant amount of monthly income (estimated at 6%), in addition to providing hedging and potentially market-beating growth. Three of the four securities selected provide high income (close to 8% to 10%), while the fourth one provides no income. Also, at times, one or more of the regular securities will be replaced with the hedging security, the Treasury fund (with much less income). So, on average, we expect the portfolio income to be roughly 6%.

Rotation Method: There are four primary securities, each being allocated 25%. Every month, we will calculate the total performance returns (including any distributions) over the previous three months and seven months for each of the four securities, as well as for the fallback security. We will assign 50% weightage to each of the two periods (3-month and 7-month performance) to calculate the average performance. If the performance of any of the primary securities is lower than the risk-free return provided by Cash (or 1-3 month Treasury), then the primary security is replaced by the Treasury fund (IEF – the mid-term Treasury, in our example) for the next month; otherwise, it is retained. This process is repeated at the end of each month.

The four securities plus the fallback security are:

  • (ADX) Adams Diversified Equity Fund
  • (RQI) Cohen&Steers Quality Income Realty
  • (KYN) Kayne Anderson MLP Investment Closed Fund
  • (QLD) ProShares Ultra QQQ
  • The fallback security: (IEF) 7-10 Year Treasury fund
