We admit, this rotational method is a bit complex and may not suit everyone.

Concluding Thoughts

We have had an incredible run so far in 2025, after the collapse back in April. Will the bull run continue for the rest of the year? A lot depends on where the interest rates go from here. The market has baked in at least two rate cuts this year, especially after the last jobs report.

Markets will always remain uncertain. So, it is advisable to invest regularly. Even if you have a lump sum or a large chunk of money to invest, it is better to invest gradually over a period of time.

In this article, we have presented three strategies to serve the needs of a wider audience. What you may choose should be based on your individual needs, preferences, and risk tolerance. One can also combine these three strategies to form a really good SWAN-type portfolio.

data.title: BTCS: I've Changed My Mind (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I initiated coverage of BTCS Inc (NASDAQ:BTCS) for Seeking Alpha a little over a month ago given the company's pivot to a digital asset treasury business. At that time, I rated the stock a 'hold' and specifically mentioned dilution as a concern because of some of the language in the ATM prospectus. Most notably that BTCS wouldn't give specifics regarding how much capital from the ATM would be utilized for Ethereum (ETH-USD) purchases. Since then, we have a Q2 earnings report to assess as well as clearer data regarding ETH acquisitions. In this update, we'll look at that data and I'll explain why I think an upgrade is warranted despite the risks.

Q2 Earnings

For the quarter ended June, BTCS reported $2.8 million in top line revenue - all from Blockchain infrastructure operations. The company separates its blockchain ops revenue into two different buckets; NodeOps and Builder+. NodeOps derives revenue from natively staking assets on-chain as well as through delegation. Builder+ works a bit different as BTCS is purchasing block space and then bundling mempool transactions that it then proposes to validators. This is how the company explains the process for Ethereum in the 10-Q:

Revenue recognition for these activities, conducted through Builder+, entails the recognition of gas fees (or “transaction fees”) and priority fees (or “tips”) earned in exchange for successfully constructing blocks of bundled transactions and having these blocks selected and proposed by a validator to the Ethereum network for validation and successfully finalized on the network.

The breakout between NodeOps and Builder+ for both the quarter ended and six months ended June looks like this:

At 86% of blockchain ops revenue so far this year, Builder+ is by far the biggest driver of BTCS's top line. NodeOps generates income from multiple different smart contract chains including Ethereum, Cosmos (ATOM-USD), Solana (SOL-USD), NEAR Protocol (NEAR-USD) and Axie Infinity (AXS-USD). Builder+ generated revenue primarily from ETH with $3.5 million of the $3.9 million Builder+ revenue this year coming from Ethereum; the remainder came from BNB Chain (BNB-USD).

While Builder+ drives top-line, the margins for these businesses are quite different according to the 10-Q:

Revenue H1 2025 H1 2024
NodeOps $602,263 $903,692
Builder+ $3,858,870 $108,886
Cost of Rev H1 2025 H1 2024
NodeOps $60,021 $99,367
Builder+ $4,361,771 $230,106
Margin H1 2025 H1 2024
NodeOps 90.03% 89.00%
Builder+ -13.03% -111.33%

Source: BTCS, 10-Q

The margins for NodeOps are fantastic. Margins for Builder+, while much better in 2025, indicate that the segment actually loses money. The company says that the cost of revenues for Builder+ includes node maintenance employee salaries as well as infrastructure hosting. In any case, it looks as though BTCS Inc would be better off doing nothing at all and sitting on its ETH rather than consolidating transactions - at least in a low-fee environment.

As I understand the model, the company purchases block space through 'a fixed non-negotiable' fee paid to network validators. This could theoretically make transaction bundling profitable for BTCS in a higher-fee environment. But we'll have to see how that plays out should Ethereum ever return to a fee-growth network.

ETH Treasury and AAVE Strategy

Subsequent to earnings, BTCS has disclosed owning over 70k ETH. At current Ethereum prices, the company's ETH holdings have a market value of just under $300 million:

Yet, the market cap of just $208.5 million seemingly presents an arbitrage. With most of the other 'ETH Treasury' companies in the equity market trading at premiums to NAV, BTCS trades at a 30% discount to ETH holdings. Screaming buy? It's a bit more complicated than that.

Subsequent to the company's earnings report and ETH purchases, BTCS borrowed an additional $47 million from lending protocol AAVE (AAVE-USD):

As of August 12, 2025, the Company had approximately $51,702,000 in outstanding borrowings, inclusive of accrued interest, collateralized by approximately 38,400 ETH with a fair market value of approximately $176,062,000, based on the ETH closing price of $4,584 on that date.

It would be a straightforward arbitrage to buy BTCS at a 30% discount to holdings if the ETH was natively staked and earning passive yield. However, I think the discount for BTCS is likely due to concerns about the company's AAVE borrowings even though BTCS's AAVE loan is over-collateralized. Admittedly, AAVE yields have moved against BTCS since it took out its latest loans:

At present, borrowing Tether (USDT-USD) through AAVE has an APY of 6.5% percent. During the time period that BTCS took out the loans, AAVE USDT APYs were closer to 5.5%. Meaning, BTCS has already seen its borrowing cost increase in less than two weeks. The reason for this spike in borrowing cost is because the amount of USDT available to borrow through AAVE has fallen from $1.4 billion on August 1st to just $442 million as of article submission. But as yields from borrowing increase, so too does the yield from providing liquidity. Thus, I expect AAVE's USDT borrow rates may actually come back down as more liquidity chases dollar-denominated yield that exceed the rate of t-bills.

Even though BTCS is staking the ETH to generate yield, staking rewards from the Ethereum blockchain have fallen below 3%. So on the surface, the company is borrowing at 6.5% to stake (or lend, if that helps) at 3% while the value of the asset being staked may have peaked temporarily. But even still, I think the discount for BTCS shares is too extreme given what the company has actually been doing.

The table below is my rough estimate at what the company would generate in earnings given current borrow cost, supply cost, and staking yield:

ETH Strategy ETH $ Value Rate Annual
Pledged ETH 38,400 $163,776,000 2.30% $3,766,848
Purchased with Borrowed USDT 16,100 $68,666,500 2.92% $2,005,062
Borrowed USDT -$52,000,000 6.50% -$3,380,000
Net Earnings From ETH Strategy $2,391,910

Source: BTCS, Analyst's estimates at $4,265 ETH price

Even at sub-3% staking yields, 6.5% USDT borrow cost, and 2.3% from collaraterlized ETH, this strategy is well in the black by my math. In fact, even if ETH fell to $3,500 and USDT borrow costs through AAVE rose to 9%, this strategy would still be slightly profitable.

So what is BTCS worth?

I think the right way to look at BTCS valuation is 'net ETH' rather than the market cap to Treasury holdings metrics that we generally see with these kinds of companies.

BTCS 'net ETH' Calculation
Total ETH 70,140
Loan $ Value $52,000,000
Loan in ETH 12,381
Net ETH 57,759
$ Value at $4,200 $242,588,000
Shares outstanding 48,000,000
Net ETH Per Share $5.05
True Discount 14.72%

Source: BTCS Inc, Analyst's estimates

The math in the table above shows a hypothetical situation in which BTCS sells $52 million in ETH to pay off its AAVE loan. This would require 12,381 ETH at current prices, leaving almost 58k ETH and a gain of nearly 4k ETH on the loan. It also indicates that at an ETH price of $4,200 per coin, BTCS has a 'net ETH' value slightly above $5 per share.

Key Risks

As I mentioned in my first piece covering BTCS shares, dilution remains a major concern for BTCS shareholders. We've already seen shares outstanding explode since the end of Q2:

Furthermore, the company had a small fraction of the ETH it currently has when I last covered the company in July yet the share price is essentially flat.

Something else to consider is Short Interest in this stock is getting aggressive with nearly 14% of shares outstanding currently sold short. This is a larger level of short interest currently than what we see in other ETH Treasury companies like SharpLink Gaming (SBET) or Bitmine Immersion (BMNR). From where I sit, it's hard to blame the bears for this level of conviction given BTCS's history of operating losses.

Closing Thoughts

This may come as a surprise to some of my readers, but I'm actually much less pessimistic about BTCS at this point in time. That change in sentiment is mainly due to the equity valuation relative to the ETH held in reserve. Sure, some of this has been pledged as collateral. But given the company's collateralization ratio, ETH would likely have to fall below $2,000 per coin before any loan liquidation fears would manifest. I don't think that's a concern short of an economic calamity akin to COVID.

If we observe the company's valuation through the net ETH metric, BTCS shares are trading at a discount and should likely be closer to $5. I've changed my mind on BTCS. I actually think it's an interesting ETH Treasury spec on its AAVE strategy alone. Factor in that it's trading at a discount to net ETH value, and I think it's one of the better ways to play ETH treasuries in the market right now. To be sure, the negative margins from Builder+ are a concern. But those losses are more than made up for by the spread from the company's AAVE protocol usage. Short Interest is high and growing. I don't mind being a contrarian here. In my view, BTCS is a 'buy.'

data.title: U.S. Economy: Recent Data Suggests Risk To Earnings
data.description:
The latest economic data suggests the US economy is decelerating. That means growth is slowing, jobs are shrinking, and households are spending less. As we showed in a recent #BullBearReport, economic growth, inflation, and personal consumption are trending lower.

Unsurprisingly, with job growth weakening, consumer sentiment also took a hit in the latest report, with future expectations remaining very weak.

While the financial markets got very excited over the data, as it creates pressure for rate cuts in the US, it bodes poorly for future returns. So far in 2025, there have been 88 central bank rate cuts, the fastest cut cycle since the 2020 COVID crash. That is not a sign of burgeoning economic growth. While stocks and credit anticipate the Fed joining the easing party, the pace of cuts suggests that weakness is spreading globally.

Crucially, economic growth, or the lack thereof, shapes earnings expectations and investor behavior. At first glance, it looked like the US economy was regaining strength in the latest GDP report. But the drivers of that growth tell a different story. Consumer spending rose 1.4 percent, the primary contributor. However, this increase was funded mainly through savings depletion and increased credit usage. Real wage growth remained stagnant, which means consumers were not spending more because they earned more. They borrowed and spent their savings.

Another major contributor to Q2 GDP was a sharp drop in imports. While this technically boosts GDP, it is not indicative of economic health. Imports fell because domestic demand weakened, and tariffs continued to distort trade flows. Export growth was modest, but not enough to offset the larger picture of global trade deceleration. Private fixed investment also declined in Q2, suggesting businesses are pulling back on capital expenditures, particularly equipment and structures. This signals reduced confidence in future demand. Inventory restocking added marginally to GDP, but with backlogs thinning and sales slowing, inventory reductions are likely in Q3 and Q4.

Furthermore, while investors are exceedingly bullish on the stock market, forecasts for the remainder of 2025 are sobering. Most projections now place US economic growth at 1.5 percent or lower in the second half. By Q4, real GDP growth could slow to below 1.0 percent year-over-year. Even the IMF, which recently produced its global growth estimates, has the US economy growing at 2% for the next two years and the Eurozone near 1%.

As discussed above, consumer spending, the primary driver of GDP, will be constrained by slowing wage growth, high debt servicing costs, and reduced access to credit. The economy lacks alternative growth engines without a pickup in business investment or export demand. That reduction in economic demand will translate into forward earnings expectations, requiring a revaluation of financial markets.

Let’s look at some of the most recent economic data to assess the risks.

US Economic Data: Good, Not Great

July’s employment report confirmed that the slowdown in US economic growth is taking root. Nonfarm payrolls increased by a mere 73,000 jobs. Expectations were for 110,000. The bigger story is in the revisions. May and June payrolls were slashed by 258,000. That’s the second-largest two-month downward revision since the 2008 recession. When employers stop hiring and previous gains get erased, that is not a data glitch—it’s a signal.

The unemployment rate increased to 4.2 percent, while labor force participation remained at 62.2 percent. While not alarming at face value, the lack of participation growth shows that the working-age population isn’t reentering the workforce in significant numbers. More importantly, full-time employment remains weak as a percentage of total employees, which correlates to the future direction of personal consumption expenditures and inflation. Such is unsurprising given that for individuals to consume at higher levels, they need full-time employment. Without increases in consumption, US economic growth and inflation will decline.

Wage growth is also losing momentum. Average hourly earnings rose 0.3 percent month-over-month, but year-over-year gains slowed to 3.9 percent. This is a problem. Inflation is still hot in services and necessities, eroding real income growth.

Consequently, households feel the pinch, and discretionary spending will reflect that in the second half of 2025. Such is particularly the case with rising delinquency rates, particularly in student loan debt.

The sector breakdown of job growth shows late-cycle dynamics. Healthcare and government jobs are still being added, but these are non-cyclical. Manufacturing employment declined, reflecting weaker orders and global trade slowdowns. Professional and business services hiring has stalled. Furthermore, the labor market is transitioning from a phase of post-pandemic recovery to a structurally slower employment cycle. Businesses are tightening labor expenses as revenue growth slows.

This is not a short-term adjustment; it’s a reset of hiring expectations across multiple sectors. If wage growth continues to moderate and hiring slows, the consumption-driven economy will lose its primary growth engine.

Data Reveals Broad-Based Slowdown

Outside the employment report, July’s ISM Manufacturing Index fell to 48.0, marking five months of contraction. New orders were sluggish, while the employment index plummeted to 43.4 percent. This reading is significant. It indicates companies are actively reducing factory jobs, not simply slowing hiring. The problem is not isolated to manufacturing. The services sector, comprising roughly 70 percent of the U.S. economy, barely holds on. The ISM Services Index registered at 50.1 in July, down from a reading of 50.8 previously. This is technically an expansion reading, but only by the slimmest margin.

The composite index is economically weighted (70% services / 30% manufacturing). It tells us what we already suspected: that the economy is growing, albeit very slowly.

The employment component of services fell to 46.4 percent. When service firms start cutting payrolls, it signifies a more profound weakness in demand. For months, services spending was the buffer against manufacturing softness. That buffer is eroding as input costs have risen. Businesses face increasing operational expenses while revenue growth stagnates, a recipe for margin compression.

This was evident already in the Q2 earnings reporting period. There would have been no earnings growth without Megacap Technology and major Wall Street banks.

Industrial companies have already started guiding lower. Tariffs, higher input costs, and weakening orders are cutting into margins. Companies like Caterpillar, Deere, and 3M have issued profit warnings. Consumer-facing companies are also vulnerable. Discretionary spending is slowing, and rising input costs will further squeeze margins for retailers and service providers.

While technology and AI-driven firms have recently become bright spots, their strength cannot offset broader corporate margin pressures. In Q2, S&P 500 earnings grew 6.4%, with 80 percent of companies beating estimates. But this masks a weakening breadth of growth, where earnings beats are concentrated in essentially just two sectors.

As noted, slowing economic growth will reduce earnings expectations for Q3 and Q4. Margin compression will dominate as companies with pricing power and efficient cost structures prosper while the rest struggle to meet expectations.

Investors must now consider this risk.

Investor Positioning in a Slowing Economy

The evidence points to a slowing US economy. Growth is weakening, inflation remains elevated, corporate margins face pressure, and interest rate cuts are likely. These conditions require a shift in investment strategy. Investors must adapt to preserve capital, generate income, and manage risk. Positioning should emphasize resilience, quality, and income stability. The goal is to reduce exposure to volatile sectors and concentrate on assets that perform well during economic slowdowns.

Here are key actions investors should consider:

  • Reduce exposure to cyclical stocks: Cut back on discretionary sectors like retail, travel, and consumer electronics that rely heavily on strong economic growth.
  • Increase allocation to defensive sectors: Focus on consumer staples, healthcare, and utilities. These sectors provide stable earnings even in weak environments.
  • Favor companies with strong pricing power: These firms can better maintain margins despite rising input costs.
  • Prioritize strong balance sheets: Low debt and high cash reserves reduce financial stress and support consistent returns.
  • Add high-quality dividend payers: Look for companies with a track record of stable or growing dividends. These provide income support as capital gains slow.
  • Increase fixed income exposure: Short-duration bonds and high-grade corporates may benefit from falling interest rates.
  • Consider yield curve positioning: A steeper yield curve from rate cuts may create an opportunity in intermediate bonds.
  • Avoid speculative growth stocks. These firms rely on future earnings and cheap financing, both of which are under pressure in a slowing economy.

A decelerating US economy changes the return profile across asset classes. Adjusting now to focus on quality, cash flow, and defensive positioning can improve downside protection and set the stage for more stable portfolio returns.

While there are no guarantees, the current gap between what Wall Street expects and what the economy can deliver is very different. Could the economy catch up to meet Wall Street’s expectations? Sure. It just usually doesn’t happen that way.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Enphase Energy: Bear Case Weakens, Why I'm Upgrading To Hold
data.description:
In my previous Enphase Energy (NASDAQ:ENPH) article, I had rated the company a sell, mainly due to strong macroeconomic headwinds. Demand looked soft. Policy risk felt heavy, and the U.S. floated ending the tax credits Enphaseleans on. That combo mattered, because credits do not just boost quarterly optics, they shape pricing, factory mix, even how much risk a management team will take. When subsidies wobble, investor time horizons shrink. You felt that in the tape.

Since then a few things have changed. Q2 2025 came in better than expected, which tells me end demand is stabilizing rather than sliding. Margins held up, helped by credits, so the headline looks fine, but I still read that as assisted strength, not pure muscle. Treasury guidance also landed milder than many feared, which takes some tail risk off the table. Put that next to a stock that is already down a lot year over year, and you get a valuation that no longer lives in the clouds. I think the bear case lost some teeth.

What might happen next? If orders keep inching up, and if core margins hold as the quarter rolls forward, the market will likely give Enphase more room; you can see how that feeds a grind higher. If rates bite again or policy tightens, we are probably back to a choppy range. For now I am not ready to call it a buy. I am comfortable moving to Hold. Better setup, still real headwinds.

2nd Quarter Earnings and 3rd Quarter Guidance

Let’s start off by going through Enphase’s Q2 FY2025 results, as they actually surprised to the upside. Revenue came in at $363.2 million, up ~19.6% year-over-year from $303.5 million, driven by higher microinverter and battery shipments. Most notably, management indicated the dreaded inventory glut has finally cleared as CEO Badri Kothandaraman said “[they] have completed inventory normalization in the U.S. and Europe in Q2,”, which paves the way for more predictable real-time demand.

In fact, he saw signs that the U.S. residential solar market is “gradually recovering”, with Q2 installer sell-through better than Q1, hinting that the worst of the slowdown may be over.

Overall, Enphase’s international business remains a bright spot as well. European revenue rose about 11% in Q2, thanks to robust sales of the new IQ Battery 5P and expanded product adoption. Key markets like the Netherlands, France, and Germany were highlighted as strong performers. This geographic balance helped offset a still-recovering U.S. market. Enphaseshipped 1.53 million microinverters in the quarter (675 MW DC worth) and a record 190.9 MWh of batteries, which shows there’s still robust storage demand.

Enphase’s profitability also held up better than anticipated. Non-GAAP gross margin was 48.6%, boosted by U.S. incentives. However, it’s important to note this was heavily bolstered by government incentives. Enphaseis taking advantage of the 45X advanced manufacturing tax credit by producing microinverters in the U.S., which contributed an 11.4% net benefit to Q2 gross margin. In other words, without these IRA subsidies, underlying gross margin would have been in the high-30s percentage range. Management confirmed this dynamic on the call: reciprocal tariffs on imported components shaved about 2 percentage points off Q2 margin, but the IRA production credits more than offset those costs. After factoring out roughly $41.5 million in net IRA benefits for the quarter, Enphase’s core margin is much thinner than the headline suggests.

In terms of guidance, Enphase’s expectations for Q3 2025 are riddled with caution. Enphaseforecast revenues in the $330–$370 million area, which would be a slight sequential decline. The soft outlook aligns with a solar market that is still regaining its footing after years of high interest rates. More importantly, Enphaseguided for a gross margin of about 43%–46% (non-GAAP) in Q3, which is a step down from Q2 levels. There is really only one major reason for the headwinds: excluding IRA credits, underlying gross margin would only be ~33%–36% for Q3, which is really far from past levels. The difference is largely due to tariff and cost pressures, plus a lower projected IRA credit ($34–$38M in Q3 vs $41.5M in Q2 given fewer U.S.-made unit shipments), which doesn’t exactly lower volatility.

Policy Tailwinds and Updates

A major overhang in our prior Sell thesis was U.S. policy risk. After the 2024 elections, the new administration and Congress moved to curb renewable energy subsidies via the “One Big Beautiful Bill Act” (OBBBA). Initially, there was fear that the Treasury would set extremely tight requirements effectively slamming the brakes on new solar projects. However, the recent guidance from the U.S. Treasury turned out milder than expected, offering a bit of relief for the industry. At least for now.

So what happened? The IRS released new “beginning of construction” rules (Notice 2025-42) in mid-August. The 5% spending safe-harbor rule, which previously let developers qualify for tax credits by incurring at least 5% of project costs before the deadline, was eliminated for large projects. Instead, projects now truly have to start physical construction to lock in their credits. Another important point is that smaller projects under 1.5 MW can still use the old 5% cost safe harbor method. This 1.5 MW threshold covers most residential and small commercial solar installations, which is Enphase’s core market. This should help avoid a sudden cliff in Enphase’s residential segment as the policy winds down.

From Enphase’s perspective, the Treasury guidance was about as good an outcome as one could given the previous communications from the government. It largely preserves solar projects’ access to funding. The guidance also left in place incentives that directly aid Enphase. Notably, the 45X manufacturing credit for U.S.-built solar components remains intact, aligning with reshoring policies. Enphasehas leaned heavily into U.S. production to capture these credits, so their continuation could be saving grace.

Now, the bad news is that the OBBBA did impose new challenges that could still pose longer-term headwinds for Enphaseand the rest of the industry. For instance, the law restricts solar panels with overseas materials from qualifying, which significantly limits developers’ access to cheaper, imported modules. In theory, this could raise costs for utility-scale solar farms and slow their deployment. And moreover, any solar projects coming online after 2027 won’t get the federal credits. These uncertainties temper the long-term outlook, but in the immediate term, the policy changes are not as bad as it was initially thought.

Performance Grades

Enphase used to trade at a pretty elevated valuation, but today it sits closer to the rest of the sector. Forward P.E at 13.35 and trailing non GAAP P.E at 11.77 are roughly forty percent below the sector. EV to Sales around 2.9, and forward EV to EBITDA near 9.8, also come in under the medians. I think that says the market already priced a slower phase, and investors moved from premium to show me.

The wrinkle is forward EV to EBIT at 36.58, almost double the group. That points to worries about EBIT conversion, either softer operating leverage, or more D and A as U.S. manufacturing ramps. Price to book is still a touch rich, while price to cash flow sits below peers, which fits a cash is fine, growth is the worry read. Versus five year averages most multiples are lower, so yes, cheaper, but not a layup. If core margins firm and opex stays tight, this gap can close. If not, it probably trades near fair value and reacts to headlines.

Profitability remains the bright spot, even with cracks. Gross margin has slipped to 32.77%, well below the sector near 50%, which is the main weak read. Everything lower down the P&L looks better. EBIT margin at 12.82% and EBITDA margin at 17.79% both sit comfortably above peers, and the net margin at 11.78% is strong too. Levered FCF margin at 20.73% stands out, and return metrics still look healthy, with ROE at 19.79% far ahead of the sector. Cash from operations was $412.47M over the last twelve months, and cash per share at 2.83 is also ahead.

The two soft spots are that asset turnover is only 0.47x, and gross margin is well under the sector and under its own 5-year average, so mix and pricing still matter a lot. I think the read here is simple. Enphase can still translate demand into cash and earnings when volumes cooperate, and that gives a cushion, but the cushion depends on holding line on costs and gross margin.

Growth is where the grade falls apart. Revenue was up only 4.39% year over year, below the sector, and the forward line points negative at minus 14.75%. The trailing operating rebound looks good on paper, EBITDA up 55.70% and EBIT up 93.27%, but forward estimates swing back down, with EBITDA and EBIT both projected to contract, and GAAP EPS shows a sharp decline both trailing and forward. Long-term EPS growth is basically flat at minus 0.41%, which explains the lost premium in the valuation picture.

There are a few pockets of strength, like levered FCF growth up 26.27% and positive operating cash flow growth, but even those flip negative on the forward line. Working capital and capex are contracting, which reads like a company tightening sails while demand resets. I think the message here is clear. Profitability is still respectable, the balance sheet throws off cash, but the growth engine needs a restart. Until forward growth turns up, the market will keep Enphase in the penalty box on the growth grade, and it will show up in those mixed forward valuation signals.

Options chain analysis

EnphaseEnergy has been a frustrating stock for investors the past year. The shares are down sharply from the $70s into the $30s, and each bounce has been sold. With Q2 numbers behind us and subsidy rules shifting in the U.S., I wanted to take a closer look at what the options market is saying right now. The data helps frame where risk and opportunity sit over the next few weeks.

Spot is near $36.02, boxed between zero GEX at $35.50 and a call wall at $37. That is a classic pin. Dealers are long gamma in that pocket, which usually means smaller moves and mean reversion intraday. The biggest negative GEX sits down at $30, which lines up with the put support line. If the stock slips below $35.50 and cannot reclaim it, I would expect flows to start pulling toward $35 first, then $34, with $30 as the true pain point. The right side of the chart shows heavy positive GEX right at $37 and $36.50. If price pushes through $37 and holds, hedging pressure works with you, not against you, and that opens the door to a grind toward $40.00.

The closest expiry, August 22, shows zero GEX at $34 and a call wall at $36. That again brackets spot. Dealers will dampen swings into that Friday. The second closest expiry, August 29, shifts the call wall up to $40 and keeps zero GEX near $34. That matters. It says the market is willing to entertain upside outside the immediate week if price can stabilize. The Sept 5 panel strengthens that. Zero GEX drops to $32, call wall sits at $37, and positive GEX stacks above spot. In simple words. Pin this week, more air above in late August and early September if the tape cooperates.

When it comes to open interest, I see concentration at $30, $35, and $40. That is essentially your anchor, magnet, and ceiling. You can see why the stock keeps gravitating to the mid 30s. Calls and puts are both thick at $35, and there is a real wall at $40 with large call open interest. Until contracts roll or price shocks through, these levels will steer the path of least resistance.

The single day column shows August 22 at about plus 4.1 million net GEX, with the cumulative column running about 6.6 million. That is stabilizing. It supports trends by smoothing volatility. The outlier is September 19 at about negative 2.7 million. That is the first date where dealer positioning flips into short gamma territory in size. Put it on your calendar, as we could see some volatility the closer to this expiration we get, if spot price moves towards the put support level.

When it comes to unusual options activity calls bought at $42, $43, $46, and $49 are clearly speculative. Cheap lottery options, far from spot without much delta or gamma attached to them. Puts are closer to the money, concentrated at $30, $28, $25, and $24. That looks like real hedging, rather than speculative positions. So in my opinion the split is this: traders are nibbling on upside lottos, while larger hands keep downside protection closer to the stock. I treat that as early curiosity on the long side, not a broad turn yet.

Net call premium flipped positive into mid to late August, while net put premium eased. Volumes back that up. You had notable bursts in call volume around August 15. Price briefly popped toward the high $36s and then drifted. Premiums are money, not just contracts. Dollars moved toward calls, which fits the idea of a short term pin followed by a possible push higher if resistance gives way.

Looking at what is happening with dark pools, August 18 and 19 show buy ratios of roughly 1.51 and 1.40, with DIX around 0.60 and 0.58, and the short moving averages trending higher. Generally, dark pools have been buying aggressively since July, with only a few days of net selling (likely trimming positions). You do not need to overfit it, but when DIX sits above about 0.55 for several sessions, the tape usually grinds up or at least stabilizes.

In terms of risk appetite, the skew panel below shows the 25 delta risk reversal at about negative 1.70 percent, meaning traders are generally willing to pay a premium for calls over puts. The bias is overall not very significant, and no where near an area where I would think “this is a clear buy or sell signal” kind of situation. However, the 30 day median has moved up into put territory, which could potentially be a signal that put appetite could start turn into call appetite. Still too early to tell though.

The bigger picture still matters. Enphase has ground lower from the $70s into the $30s. Every bounce has been sold for months. That is why levels like $37 and $40 matter more than usual. A push through those is not just a line on a chart. My own model still suggests that we are in sell or hold territory, as momentum for Enphase still needs to turn around, and the downtrend to be broken.

Putting it together, I think Enphase is set for a near-term pin between $35 and $37 into August 22. If $37 breaks cleanly, then $38 to $40 is the next zone with a real chance of being tested as the August 29 call wall sits higher. Downside risk is anchored at $35 and $34, with $30 the big magnet if things unravel. The next true volatility window is September 19, when dealer positioning flips negative.

So my base case is a quiet week followed by an attempt higher into late August. The bearish case is a loss of $35.50 that drags the stock back toward $34. The bullish case is a break and hold above $37, opening the door to a grind toward $40. I will be watching how the call walls migrate, and whether skew and premium flows keep favoring the long side.

Risks

Even with the improvements noted, Enphase still faces non-trivial risks and headwinds that keep us from turning bullish. Competitive and market pressures persist and growth in the residential solar market remains vulnerable to higher interest rates and evolving net metering policies. Enphase’s main rival, SolarEdge, as well as newer entrants, continue to vie for market share. The company’s own Q3 guidance reflects a muted near-term outlook, suggesting that demand, while stabilizing, is not about to re-accelerate sharply.

Margin compression is another concern. As discussed, Enphase’s impressive gross margins are propped up by temporary tax credits. The company estimated its Q3 non-GAAP gross margin ex-credits will be only ~33–36%, a level that may not satisfy investors. Tariffs on foreign-made components are currently a ~4% drag on margins. Conversely, the 45X credit that’s padding margins now is scheduled to phase out by the early 2030s. The looming expiration of solar tax credits. Both at the utility scale and eventually residential. This creates longer-term uncertainty for the entire industry, forcing future adjustments.

Policy and regulatory risks therefore remain. The current U.S. administration has shown an inclination to tighten solar incentives. Outside the U.S., Enphasemust navigate different regulatory environments, e.g. changes to European solar subsidies or grid rules could impact demand for its products in those markets.

Conclusion

Enphase is no longer the same clear sell I saw earlier this year. Q2 showed stabilization in demand and the worst of the inventory glut looks behind us. Margins still lean heavily on subsidies, and guidance confirms that underlying profitability is much thinner than the headline. Policy risk also has not gone away, but the latest Treasury rules came in softer than expected, which takes out some of the immediate tail risk.

Enphase's stock price has already been cut in half, valuation multiples now sit closer to peers, and cash generation remains solid. That combination weakens the bear case. At the same time, growth is still muted, margins are fragile without credits, and competition in residential solar is heating up. Those factors make it too early to pivot bullish.

For me, the balance has shifted. The downside case has lost some teeth, but the upside still needs proof. That is why I am moving from Sell to Hold.

data.title: Beyond: Bed Bath & Beyond Attempts To Revive Its 'Apes' As Liquidity Collapses
data.description:
Beyond's (BYON) rally from its 52-week lows partially reflects a broader return of market euphoria that has likely pushed the company's management to revive Bed Bath & Beyond ("BBBY"). BYON's name and ticker on the NYSE are set to change to that of the once-bankrupt retailer, whose demise I warned readers about in December 2022. Under the weight of its substantial debt burden, BBBY filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in the spring of 2023, with BYON acquiring the brand out of bankruptcy. BYON has pursued a strategy of acquiring the intellectual property of failed brands, including online discount women's and apparel retailer Zulily in 2024.

In my opinion, the move is a visceral attempt to attach the fortunes of the struggling parent company of BBBY to current euphoric animal spirits. BBBY was in many ways one of the poster boys of the pandemic-era meme stock mania. This likely won't return on a ticker change, with current market excitement broadly being driven by tickers in the artificial intelligence space like CoreWeave (CRWV). There has been very little participation from the pandemic-era meme stock class, and tickers like AMC Entertainment (AMC) and BlackBerry (BB) are down year-to-date. I last covered BYON in April, flagging its dipping liquidity as a core risk factor. This risk remains and is set to be ramped up by management's plans following a revived BBBY. The company wants to convert Kirkland’s, renamed The Brand House Collective (TBHC), into Bed Bath & Beyond Home outlets. The first location opened up in Nashville in July, with BYON planning to convert another 75 Kirkland's stores through 2026.

Net Losses And The Sustained Dip In Liquidity

Beyond BYON's push into the meme-stock market, the company's underlying financials continue to realize year-over-year weakness. Revenue for the second quarter came in at $282.25 million, down 29.1% over its year-ago comp, albeit a beat on consensus estimates by $31.88 million. BYON is primarily focused on ecommerce-focused as its main selling channel and owns interests in a range of brands, including Overstock and buybuy BABY. The NYSE listing was previously under the Overstock moniker, with the online retailer still BYON's largest revenue component. The expansion of Bed Bath & Beyond Home should see CapEx increase, although the overall scope of this will be dampened by the focus on conversions of a store format that's amenable to Bed Bath & Beyond Home.

BYON's net loss during the quarter was $19.31 million, a material improvement from a loss of $42.58 million a year ago. The company has made progress with reducing its cost base, with its G&A and tech expenses falling by $9 million year-over-year on the back of soft guidance to trim fixed costs by an annualized amount of $80 million. Further, while BYON's trailing twelve-month free cash flow was negative at $113.35 million, it was a healthy upward improvement sequentially. The uncertainty for bulls here is just how much a ramp-up in conversions of Kirkland's will stall the green shoots of progress on free cash burn. The company has been aggressively dependent on equity offerings to plug prior cash shortfalls, with existing liquidity as of the end of the second quarter of $147.53 million at a more than 3-year low.

The Bullish Counter-Point

There could be an argument from bulls that a concentrated effort to bring the Bed Bath & Beyond Home outlets to the U.S. retail landscape and to grow its footprint through conversions without the legacy debt burden that bankrupted the prior iteration could drive a sustained increase in revenue and an upturn in the fortunes of BYON. BYON essentially held no long-term debt and short-term debt of just $18.46 million as of the end of its second quarter. The company has been aggressively dilutive for its capital needs with its average diluted shares outstanding ending the second quarter at 57,503,000, up roughly 25% over the last 1-year. I'll expect this to keep on rising through 2026 on Kirkland's conversion plans and against underlying operations that remain unprofitable.

Critically, my sell rating on BYON is based on the CapEx needs the conversions will require and that the liquidity base for the company to roll this out was already under a sustained dip. BYON expanded an existing credit facility it provided to Kirkland's by $5.2 million back in May. BYON also acquired the rights to the Kirkland’s brand, and as part of this strategic partnership, the newly acquired brand will have to open and operate Bed Bath & Beyond Home, and eventually buybuy BABY stores.

BYON owns roughly 40% of Kirkland's, a struggling furniture and home decor brick-and-mortar retailer whose stock price has collapsed 88% over the last 5 years. BYON flagged during its second-quarter earnings call that its core competency is not to operate retail locations, but they're essentially leaning on the management of a ticker that has overseen a significant reduction in shareholder value in the years since the pandemic. The overlay of the Bed Bath & Beyond brand with Kirkland's retail footprint could be effective, but this is uncertain. There might be a marginal boost to shareholder value creation if BBBY is able to attract more demand to existing locations. The opposite is true if CapEx does not net its expected return.

Conclusion

BYON's move to revive BBBY represents a soft attempt to revive animal spirits with the move to convert Kirkland's stores to Bed Bath & Beyond Home outlets an uncertain strategy against the losses suffered by the two underlying companies before their respective acquisition by BYON. BYON is being rated as a sell on the back of this. I'd caution against shorting as if the apes are revived, then fundamentals won't matter.

data.title: Retirement On Autopilot: My 13-Stock AI Resistant Income Portfolio
data.description:
Introduction

So far, this year, I have spent a lot of time discussing artificial intelligence ("AI"). This includes next-gen AI technologies and their disruptive power, major AI bottlenecks like water and power, and other beneficiaries.

Although some make the case that the AI trend may be overblown, I have to disagree. And that's coming from someone who often is too conservative when it comes to major technology developments, as I prefer a "wait and see" approach whenever people start to hype certain things.

I truly believe we are entering a new economic era, as dramatic as that may sound. For example, Rihard Jarc, whom I have quoted before, noted earlier this week that Meta Platforms (META) is likely to launch its AI smartglasses at a price of roughly $800, which would put it in the same price range as high-quality smartphones.

Note that Meta has been one of the best-performing companies in recent years, as it is expected to be a top AI disruptor:

While we can discuss if this could make smartphones obsolete in the future, my point is that with AI on our noses, every human with some excess capital can become all-knowing. Just imagine a world where everyone has AI glasses.

People would know everything about everything in real time. Every inch of populated areas would be filmed at all times, and we would all be connected 24/7. Can you imagine what this could mean for society?

For the market, it means huge disruption risks.

In general, technology disruption has played a major role in the lifespan of S&P 500 companies. To give you some fascinating stats:

  • In 1958, the average lifespan of an S&P 500 company was 61 years.
  • As of 2016, that number is less than 18 years.
  • Back in 2016, research believed close to 75% of S&P 500 companies would have been gone by 2027, either through means of being bought out, mergers, or bankruptcy.

The funny thing is that while the lifespan of companies declines, our lifespan increases, as innovation (and related innovation risks) has been great for society so far:

As the life expectancy of companies drops, ours is increasing. Since the beginning of the century, 50% of the children born in advanced economies can expect to leave up to 100 years old. In addition, the retirement age will certainly increase. - Garelli (2016)

However, this has a wide range of implications, as AI disruption is being supercharged, which means the gap between AI beneficiaries and losers is rising, labor market dynamics are changing, and everything related to the flow of information is changing.

It also means that our investments could be impacted. This includes portfolios of anyone who depends on income, like retirees.

That's what this article is about.

  • We will discuss the implications of AI on the economy, including potential winners and losers at different stages of AI innovation.
  • I will explain how I would build a future-proof income portfolio. As I have often said, there is no room for error when you are truly reliant on generating the most income from your investments.

AI, This Truly Could Get Very Serious

Needless to say, any future predictions come with risks, especially when dealing with disruptive forces like AI.

That said, there's enough data to work with to establish a roadmap that can give us a good idea of what we can expect going forward. That's why I spent the past few days collecting data to figure out what the next three waves of AI could mean for our portfolios.

While I am using way more than one source, the names of the three stages came from Forbes, as I really liked their approach and categorization.

Also, and this is super important, please note that not every example I will provide is a buy recommendation. I mainly bring these stocks up to give you an idea of what kind of company I am talking about.

Stage 1: Adjustments and Early Adoption

This is the stage we're in now. Depending on who you ask, it will take up to five years before we complete this stage, which is based on the rapid integration of AI into white-collar jobs, including legal assistants, financial analysts, and others who use copilots and chatbots.

For example, I used AI to check all of the legal documents for my Albanian investment. Within seconds, I got a complete contract analysis, including potential adjustments I had to make. I don't even want to know how much a professional would have charged for it...

In this stage, we're seeing the first aggressive investments in AI. This makes AI infrastructure providers huge winners. These are the companies that will build and own the data centers, allowing customers to scale this technology.

One example is CoreWeave (CRWV) or data center REITs like Equinix (EQIX).

Other winners are large companies that adopt and scale AI systems, including large Big Tech enterprises like Alphabet (GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and many others.

Many of these companies are currently battling for the best AI model. Last week, when I took the screenshot below, Google seemed miles ahead of its peers thanks to its Gemini model.

Hence, other winners are AI-related professionals, who are being offered huge paychecks from fast-growing AI companies that fear they may be left behind.

Lowers are middle-skilled workers like legal assistants, financial analysts, and consumer service representatives who can partially be replaced by the first AI models.

For example, this is what the stock price of the French Teleperformance (OTCPK:TLPFF) company looks like, which offers customer services like telemarketing:

This also means economies with a lack of skilled labor could be falling behind.

However, generally speaking, disruption risks are limited, as we are still in the "let's find out just how powerful AI can become" stage, which may change in less than three years.

Stage 2: Great Displacement (5-15 Years Duration)

As the name suggests, this is where it gets tricky for unprepared companies.

The Great Displacement is where AI systems start to gain momentum and become more independent and able to perform more than just simple Q&A tasks. For many, this could mean gloom and doom, as Forbes writes:

Over the next decade, structural unemployment will become entrenched. Entire sectors, such as logistics, retail and basic programming, will be dominated by autonomous systems. Even creative fields, once thought immune to automation, will face disruption as generative AI tools become more sophisticated. The middle class will shrink further, leading to a bifurcated society of high-skill, high-income workers and low-skill, low-income laborers. - Forbes

Essentially, this is the part where companies start to restructure their operations around "AI-enhanced" workflows, which means AI will become more involved in forecasting, decision-making, and creative processes.

Goldman Sachs believes this could result in 15% productivity gains.

As a result, the winners will be companies that advise on AI integration, including Accenture (ACN), which is the world's leading tech-focused consultant with a booming AI business to satisfy customer demand.

Losers are mid-level managers, the traditional education system, which is unlikely to be prepared for the impact AI has on future job demand, and workers (basically anyone) who struggle to adjust to the new relationship between AI and human labor.

To use the findings of Nathaniel Stone:

AI reshapes industries by 2025, displacing jobs in manufacturing, retail, and customer service while creating growth in infrastructure and hardware sectors.

Avoid industries facing 50-94% automation rates in tasks, as AI becomes a direct competitor rather than an enabler in finance, legal, and transportation sectors.

Stage 3: New Socioeconomic Paradigm (15+ Years)

This is where it gets even more serious.

If AI is truly as disruptive as one might think, it's fair to say this is the stage where the discussion becomes almost philosophical and political:

Global power dynamics will shift as emerging economies leverage AI and offshoring to ascend economically. High-income countries that fail to adapt to these shifts may experience relative decline. Meanwhile, ethical dilemmas surrounding AI governance and the tremendous power wielded by Big Tech and a few tech billionaires will come to the forefront. All these elements risk exacerbating inequality and societal fragmentation. - Forbes

As a result, this is the part where entire sectors are being reshaped. This includes legal, finance, healthcare, as AI takes over tasks like diagnosis, legal research, and entire investment strategies.

Plain and simple, the winners will be nations with the best energy and water infrastructure to support AI infrastructure, the best talent to fuel AI innovation, and the companies best able to implement these strategies.

For now, the overwhelming majority of nations are not prepared:

Of the 73 economies assessed, only five—categorized as AI pioneers—have achieved a high level of readiness. Pioneers are also out in front in skills, R&D (research and development), ecosystems, and investments. In skills, the US and Singapore stand out with robust AI talent pools, crucial for driving innovation. The US leads in investing, driven by its sophisticated capital markets and the abundance of AI unicorns. In the R&D race, Mainland China is leading in patents and AI academic papers. - BGC

Moreover, governments need to be able to mitigate labor shifts, which could be a make-or-break task for many regions. For example, just look at what happened to the Rust Belt after steel companies went overseas or how Germany's "Ruhrgebiet" started to struggle when coal plants closed.

Other losers will be almost anyone whose job can be automated. If it can be automated, odds are it will be automated.

Personally, I have mixed feelings.

While I obviously see the benefits of AI, I have no idea if the impact on society as a whole will be great. The labor disruptions will be "wild," and I don't think we have started to imagine what a world looks like where everyone is connected to endless wisdom.

Also, how reliable is AI data?

At some point, AI will both produce and feed off of its own findings, turning the Internet into an AI-dominated database of questionable quality.

Moreover, I am convinced that people continue to underestimate just how big a deal power and water are. As ambitious as all AI plans may be, without sufficient infrastructure and energy, these plans will all be delayed.

In a recent article, I called it the "failure of imagination."

Having said all of this, I remain super bullish on power/energy companies, AI infrastructure, water companies, and diversified landowners.

However, most of these have low yields.

If I were managing a retirement account, I would own many higher-yielding stocks with a yield close to 5%, which I consider the sweet spot according to my "5% Rule."

Hence, in the next part, I'll tell you how I would incorporate AI risks and opportunities when building an income portfolio with very low risks.

An AI-Proof Retirement Income Portfolio

Please note that this is just food for thought. It almost goes without saying, but I am not proposing that people build a portfolio that looks exactly like this.

My approach is simple and prioritizes blue-chip companies and master limited partnerships ("MLPs") with strong balance sheets, consistent dividend histories, and low volatility.

As you will see in a second, I picked from sectors like utilities and pipelines (which power AI data centers), data centers (which handle AI-driven data hardware), REITs (for stable rental income from non-tech properties), and tobacco (defensive with high barriers to entry).

I also included some riskier power/energy plays for additional income and because I wanted to add some AI benefits.

  • TC Energy (TRP) is a Canadian midstream company with a major footprint in natural gas pipelines with some exposure to nuclear power. It is the only midstream company with natural gas pipelines in all three North American nations. Moreover, it has a C-Corp structure (no K-1 forms) and yields 4.8%. Adjusted for spin-offs, it has hiked its dividend for 25 consecutive years and benefits from unprecedented natural gas demand growth. However, because of its pipeline structure, nearly all of its income is fee-based, meaning commodity price risks are minimal.

  • For higher pipeline income, I would buy Western Midstream (WES), which is a Master Limited Partnership that issues a K-1 form. It has a yield of roughly 9.5%, a fast-growing footprint in the Permian Basin, where it addresses the water bottleneck and works on better natural gas egress. Its distribution (MLP dividends are called "distributions") is expected to grow by mid-to-low single digits every year going forward. Both TRP and WES are AI beneficiaries with highly favorable income and risk profiles.

  • I would aggressively buy REITs if I were retired. This includes Realty Income (O), which yields roughly 5.5%, VICI Properties (VICI) with a 5.3% yield, Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR), which yields 4.5%, and Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS), which offers a 3.4% yield. All of these REITs are AI-proof, as they provide "mission-critical" infrastructure to tenants that cannot be disrupted by AI. For Realty Income, that's service-oriented retail tenants, while VICI caters to major entertainment companies. Rexford owns Southern Californian infill market industrial properties, and Equity LifeStyle owns high-quality manufactured housing communities for retirees and others.

  • Other defensive investments include Altria Group (MO), which sports a 6.2% yield and a fast-growing footprint in smoke-free alternatives. The company is seeing elevated, double-digit growth in areas like on! and steep growth rates in the non-traditional smoking market share. I expect low-to-mid single-digit annual dividend growth.

  • When it comes to data centers, I prefer companies like Comfort Systems USA (FIX), which provide HVAC and other advanced systems for new construction and maintenance operations. However, these have low yields. If I were retired, I would buy Digital Realty Trust (DLR). The company, which yields 2.9%, has more than 730 megawatts of data centers under construction, strong pricing power, and huge tailwinds from hyperscalers.

  • I would also own at least two business development companies ("BDCs"), which are companies lending to mid-market companies. While the lending business is risky due to cyclical risks, there are two standout companies with very low charge-off ratios, backed by two of the biggest private equity/lending giants in the world. These are Ares Capital Corp. (ARCC), which yields 8.6%, and Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC), with a yield of 11.4%. However, I would keep exposure subdued to these two companies due to their cyclical risks. I would put more money into Blue Owl Capital (OWL), which owns OBDC and makes most of its money from fees. It also owns a rapidly growing net lease real estate business and yields 4.7%. The company has made it its mission to be a core lender in the AI business.

  • Then, I would buy CME Group (CME), which I currently own. The world's largest futures exchange operator uses advanced tools to expand its product offerings, enjoying one of the S&P 500's top 5 margin profiles. It pays a special dividend every year, bringing its total yield to roughly 4.5%. The higher the uncertainty and volatility, the more CME benefits, making it an excellent addition to any portfolio, I think. Regardless of whether one is focused on income or not.
  • Last but not least, I would be a buyer of Topaz Energy (TPZ:CA), which owns mineral rights in Canada's mighty Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin. This means it allows others to produce oil and gas on its land in return for royalty payments. It results in free cash flow margins of more than 90%, as the company is not required to spend any capital on oil production. Currently yielding 5.5%, the company has a 66% payout ratio and a lot of room to grow, thanks to natural gas demand and pricing tailwinds. In general, I think Topaz is a great addition to almost any income-focused portfolio, as I consider it to be a superior energy stock.

All things considered, this is what a portfolio consisting of these picks could look like:

Ticker Company Yield Exposure
TRP TC Energy 4.80% 10%
WES Western Midstream 9.50% 5%
O Realty Income 5.50% 8%
VICI VICI Properties 5.30% 8%
REXR Rexford Industrial Realty 4.50% 8%
ELS Equity LifeStyle Properties 3.40% 8%
DLR Digital Realty Trust 2.90% 8%
MO Altria Group 6.20% 10%
ARCC Ares Capital Corp. 8.60% 5%
OBDC Blue Owl Capital Corp. 11.40% 5%
OWL Blue Owl Capital 4.70% 10%
CME CME Group 4.50% 10%
TPZ:CA Topaz Energy 5.50% 5%
  • Weighted average yield: 5.5%
  • Average annual dividend growth: mid-single-digit (educated estimate)

In other words, every $100,000 would provide an additional $5,500 in annual pre-tax income for investors.

On top of that, investors can add covered-call strategies like JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), diversified ETFs like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equities ETF (SCHD), or basically any other high-quality income pick to either improve diversification, enhance the yield, or boost the growth profile.

With regard to the model portfolio, the exposures are allocated to create a balanced mix that emphasizes stability and income generation while incorporating AI beneficiaries and defensive plays.

As you can see above, higher allocations (e.g., 10%) are given to blue-chip, lower-volatility names like TRP, MO, OWL, and CME, which offer consistent dividends and resilience, even though OWL has a much riskier profile than the others.

Meanwhile, REITs receive a collective 40% exposure (that's 8% each) for their stable rental income from mission-critical properties, which are unlikely to be disrupted by AI and any other secular disruptor, for that matter.

Riskier, higher-yielding positions like WES, ARCC, OBDC, and TPZ:CA are capped at 5% each to deal with cyclical or commodity-related risks. While WES has no direct commodity risks and Topaz has no CapEx, I am still keeping their exposure somewhat limited because they tend to trade like oil stocks during times of substantial commodity price headwinds.

Technically speaking, I would sleep well if WES had a 25% weighting.

Takeaway

I think it's fair to say that AI isn't hype.

After everything we have discussed in recent quarters and in this article, it seems like it's the dawn of a new economic era, whether we're ready or not.

From smartglasses to structural unemployment, the disruption could be very profound, reshape industries and labor markets, and even create large societal problems.

Hence, for income-focused investors, the key is balancing defense and offense. That's why it makes sense to own AI infrastructure beneficiaries (like pipelines and data centers), recession-proof cash cows that include REITs and utilities, and a few high-yielders with durable advantages in the pipeline and BDC industry.

The model portfolio of this article targets a 5.5% yield with mid-single-digit growth, which I expect to be enough to withstand AI's storms while also capitalizing on its tailwinds.

Bottom line is that AI will amplify winners and losers.

That's why the goal isn't to predict the exact future of AI but to be prepared for what's next and even benefit from it. Especially for income-focused investors, there's little room for error.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Jackson Hole And Labor Market Clarity
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

What's next?

Investors today are looking to Wyoming, and it's not to book a national park getaway before the summer comes to an end. The annual Jackson Hole Economic Policy Symposium hosted by the Kansas City Fed is underway, with Chairman Jerome Powell set to take the stage at 10:00 AM ET. The theme of the 2025 conference is titled "Labor Markets in Transition: Demographics, Productivity, and Macroeconomic Policy," which will come at a time when labor market indicators are being challenged and questions swirl over real-time economic data.

Snapshot: The recent massive revision to nonfarm payroll numbers not only made waves after President Trump ousted the head of the Bureau of Labor Statistics, but also sent policymakers scrambling to make sense of the new numbers. The 258K jobs that were previously thought to have been added in May and June were actually non-existent, meaning the U.S. only added 19,000 and 14,000 jobs for those two months, respectively. Another weak NFP gain of 73K was later recorded in July, indicating a broad slowdown in job growth for the overall labor market.

Typically, that would have seriously worried many Fed officials (and some are), but others are still debating root causes to determine the path forward for monetary policy. If the underlying reasons are linked to Trump's immigration policies and baby boomer retirement, it might not warrant a strong shift in monetary policy since the downturn is based on labor supply. However, if hiring fundamentals are no longer intact, it could require a bigger dose of monetary stimulus, though that could also be complicated by signs of inflation heating up in the latest CPI and PPI prints.

The big show: Fed Chair Jerome Powell is likely to share some of his thoughts on the matter this morning and what it might mean for the interest rate outlook and long-term policy goals. Wild trading can ensue following the closely watched event, like the notable Jackson Hole hangover of 2022, which saw all three of the major averages slump between 3%-4% after Powell shot down any hopes that the Fed would telegraph a pivot on its aggressive policy tightening. "Equity markets, for its part, are entering this [year's] symposium near their highs, and stocks are largely priced for further confirmation of an easing in rates in September," SA Analyst Justin Purohit noted. "That does leave investors vulnerable if Powell's speech underwhelms and delivers unexpected surprises."
