data.title: Constellium Aims For Robust Return On Capital Growth, Shares Seem Fairly Valued
data.description:
A Buy Rating for Constellium SE

First, let's say something about the profile of Constellium SE (NYSE:CSTM), what activities it conducts, and in which markets it operates.

In Constellium's report for the second quarter and first half of 2025, the company describes itself as follows. It states:

About Constellium

Constellium is a global sector leader that develops innovative, value-added aluminum products for a broad scope of markets and applications, including aerospace, packaging and automotive. Constellium generated $7.3 billion of revenue in 2024.

Seeking Alpha writes:

It sells its products directly or through distributors in France, Germany, the Czech Republic, the United Kingdom, Switzerland, and the United States, as well as South Korea and China. The company was incorporated in 2010 and is headquartered in Paris, France.

Let's take a look at the products Constellium offers in each market segment. The following screenshot shows a description from the " CSTM Company Profile" page on Seeking Alpha.

Commenting on Constellium's report for the second quarter and first half of 2025 and guidance for full year 2025, Jean-Marc Germain, Chief Executive Officer of Constellium, highlighted the following hurdles to the company's nevertheless resilient performance:

"Constellium delivered solid results in the second quarter despite continued demand weakness across most of our end markets outside of packaging. […]"

Let's Look At How The Business Areas Have Developed

Aerospace & Transportation (A&T)

The Aerospace & Transportation (A&T) segment reported lower adjusted EBITDA in the second quarter of 2025 and the first half of 2025 compared to the prior year, primarily due to lower shipments of aerospace and TID (" transportation, industry and defense") rolled products, despite favorable selling prices and a favorable product mix, including higher metal prices, which led to a revenue improvement in the second quarter of 2025.

Packaging & Automotive Rolled Products (P&ARP)

Adjusted EBITDA for the Packaging and Automotive Rolled Products (P&ARP) segment improved in the second quarter of 2025 and the first half of 2025 compared to the prior-year period primarily due to higher shipments of packaging rolled products (while automotive rolled products shipments decreased slightly), and improved on higher revenues due to higher shipments, and favorable selling prices and mixes, including higher metal prices.

Automotive Structures & Industry (AS&I)

Adjusted EBITDA for the Automotive Structures & Industry (AS&I) segment worsened in the second quarter of 2025 and the first half of 2025 compared to the prior year due to unfavorable pricing and product mix, the unfavorable net impact of tariffs, and lower shipments of automotive extrusions, despite higher revenues due to favorable selling prices and product mix, including higher metal prices.

From a Consolidated Perspective

Weaker results in the A&T, AS&I, and H&C ("Holdings and Corporate") segments, partially offset by stronger results in the P&ARP segment, are reflected in the financial metrics disclosed by the company in the following table:

The company reported the following year-over-year cash flow and free cash flow figures, which supported a return of value to shareholders by funding its own share repurchase programs: Constellium repurchased 3.4 million shares of the company for $35 million, representing 85.4% of its second-quarter 2025 free cash flow. Constellium repurchased 4.8 million shares of the company for $50 million more than its first-half 2025 free cash flow of $38 million, utilising some of its existing liquidity.

Outlook On Constellium's Share Buyback Program

Commenting on Constellium's second quarter and first half of 2025 results and full year 2025 guidance, Jean-Marc Germain, Chief Executive Officer of Constellium, said:

"While the tariff and international trade situation remains fluid, […] we are raising our guidance for 2025 and now expect

Adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $620 million to $650 million, […] and Free Cash Flow in excess of $120 million.

Our guidance assumes that the overall macroeconomic and end market environment will remain relatively stable.

We also remain confident in our ability to deliver on our long-term target of Adjusted EBITDA of $900 million, […] and Free Cash Flow of $300 million, in 2028. […]".

Adjusted EBITDA growth from 2025 to 2028 averages 12.33% (CAGR basis). This indicates that Constellium's operations are forecast to grow faster than most of its competitors, as the sector median's EBITDA growth ("YoY") stands at 7.28% and the sector median's EBITDA growth ("FWD") stands at 6.69%.

Free cash flow growth from 2025 to 2028 averages 35.72% (CAGR basis). This indicates the company is seen to be very efficient in generating returns on invested capital. Constellium's free cash flow per share is expected to grow by approximately 35.72% per share, based on 139.54 million shares outstanding (ticker). This compares to a sector median's free cash flow per share ("FWD") growth rate of 6.15%. In terms of profitability growth, Constellium is potentially a leader in the aluminium sector.

The Financial Condition: The Balance Sheet Is Quite Leveraged, But Looks Still Stable

Some Key Financial Indicators Ahead Of A Plausible Development Of Constellium's Business Segments

  • An Altman Z-score of 1.95 means the balance sheet is in a grey area, with a moderate risk of adverse future events. You can find this value in the "Risk" section of this Seeking Alpha page. However, in our view, there is no cause for concern. Constellium operates in a capital-intensive industry, and the level of debt on the balance sheet is not atypical for this kind of business.
  • Constellium SE's 12-month Interest Coverage Ratio is calculated from the 12-month operating income of $252 million ("numerator") and the 12-month interest expense of $96 million ("denominator"). This results in a value of 2.625. Investors typically aim for a value of at least 1.5 or 2.0x so that the company is considered able to meet its principal and interest payments. With this value of 2.625, Constellium is on a solid upward trend from the perspective of a prudent investor. With the investment season now over, it's time to profit from the expected strong demand in the Constellium markets and as so an improvement in the interest coverage ratio is, in our view, an inevitable consequence.
  • The Net Debt to Adjusted EBITDA Ratio is calculated as follows: Net Debt of $1,895 million as of June 30, 2025 ("numerator") and Adjusted EBITDA of $604 million for the last 12 months ending in the second quarter of 2025 ("denominator"). Adjusted EBITDA is $332 million for the first half of 2025 plus $272 million for the second half of 2024 ( full year 2024 less H1 2024), resulting in adjusted EBITDA of $604 million for the trailing 12 months to the second quarter of 2025. The net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of 3.14 could actually be better, as the aluminium industry generally has a range of 1.5 to 3.0. But the company's leverage and its ability to service its debt with operating income remain acceptable, plus its risk profile is tolerable and mitigated by the prospects for Constellium's market segments.

Outlook for Constellium's Business Segments

P&ARP remains the company's segment that ensures stable, long-term income. The company leverages the sustainability profile of products such as aluminium cans and food packaging as the world moves toward a gradual transition to a clean and environmentally friendly economy.

The A&SI segment is poised for significant growth, driven by the increasing adoption of electric vehicles and their accessories, including batteries and spare parts. As we know, the most advanced and powerful economies have prioritised the adoption of electric vehicles and their batteries as an unattainable goal in their strategies to build more sustainable and environmentally friendly economies. These vehicles will gradually replace the environmentally harmful and CO2-intensive fleet of conventional vehicles with combustion engines. At the same time, demand for attractive, functional, high-performance, and safe technologies is augmenting. Thanks to its properties, aluminium is an ideal raw material for developing vehicle aerodynamics, safety, and compliance. Aluminium is particularly well-suited for improving safety through battery enclosures and crash management systems. Constellium relies on its increased production capacity in Western markets, particularly North America and Europe, for its aluminium products for the automotive industry. The company expects robust demand from the automotive industry there, driven by electric vehicle technology. After the phase of balance sheet leverage, the expansion investments should now bear fruit.

The A&T segment resembles the characteristics of the previous two market segments: the aerospace and military sectors are expected to experience rapid growth as governments ramp up their spending on space programs and weapons production capacity. The civil aviation and transportation sector will deliver more moderate but stable long-term returns. Following the recovery from the disruptions caused by the COVID-19 pandemic, a period of sustained growth should follow. Currently, travel demand is still suffering, as consumer purchasing power needs time to adjust to three-year inflation. Furthermore, the credit crunch has reduced family budgets for vacations and travel. But this is only temporary until more people start travelling again, creating better conditions for an even more stable demand for civil aircraft and means of transportation in general, as well as various repair and maintenance services. As a key supplier to the civil aviation and transportation industries, Constellium can only benefit.

Stock Price: Shares Do Not Seem Low, But The Valuation May Indicate Otherwise

At the time of writing, Constellium SE's NYSE-listed shares, trading under the ticker symbol "CSTM", were trading at $14.36 per share, representing a market capitalisation of $2.04 billion.

The shares traded above the moving average lines and also above the $12.3 midpoint of the 52-week range of $7.33 to $17.27 per share.

However, the RSI of 55.60 does not indicate an overbought level; we are still far from that. It is always advisable to buy shares when they are as low as possible (usually correlated with an RSI near 30 or oversold levels), as this strategy mitigates the negative impact of this stock's low liquidity. Seeking Alpha in the "Trading Data" section shows that Constellium shares listed on the NYSE have an average (3-month) volume of 1.50 million, which is not much as an average number of shares traded daily.

But based on the following fundamental valuation compared to the sector median, Constellium appears to be fairly valued in the market, and in our view, it would be reasonable if potential buyers move up from these price levels.

Constellium has raised its 2025 guidance and now expects adjusted EBITDA between $620 million and $650 million and free cash flow in excess of $120 million. Assuming adjusted EBITDA grows at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 12.33% between 2025 and 2028 and free cash flow grows at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 35.72% between 2025 and 2028 (based on earlier mentioned long-term company guidance), the estimated adjusted EBITDA for 2026 is $713.21 million, and free cash flow for 2026 is $162.86 million.

Free cash flow per share is expected to be at least $1.17 in 2026, as 139.54 million is the number of shares outstanding. Since Constellium SE's NYSE-listed shares, "CSTM", traded at $14.36 per share, the price-to-per-share free cash flow ("FWD") ratio is 12.27 times. As an indicator, this is not a bad number and could suggest that Constellium's shares are fairly valued, although we should add here that conservative investors generally like to see a price-to-earnings-per-share free cash flow ("FWD") ratio that does not exceed 10x.

Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be at least $713.21 million in 2026. As Constellium SE's NYSE-listed shares, "CSTM", had an Enterprise Value of $4.08 billion at the time of this writing, the EV-to-adjusted EBITDA ("FWD") ratio is 5.72x versus the Sector Median's EV-to-adjusted EBITDA ("FWD") ratio of 8.79x. Constellium shares appear undervalued compared to many of its peers in the aluminium sector relative to this fundamental valuation metric.

Conclusion

This analysis concludes that a buy recommendation is appropriate for the NYSE-listed shares of Constellium SE. The company's balance sheet is highly leveraged due to investments in increased production capacity to meet demand in certain strategic markets expected to boost Constellium's sales and profitability in the coming years. This is particularly true for electric vehicles and batteries in the automotive industry, but other markets within packaging, aviation and transportation will also be good drivers in light of the augmented public awareness of the use of environmentally friendly products and as households return to spending as they did before inflation and rising interest rates to curb the inflation.

Despite the highly leveraged balance sheet, Constellium does not have any problem in taking on its debt and the resulting financial obligations, including the payment of interest expenses.

Constellium has issued guidance with higher adjusted EBITDA and FCF for 2025 and up to 2028. Relative to future adjusted EBITDA and FCF, Constellium shares now appear to be fairly valued, and conservative investors may potentially view this as a buy signal.

data.title: Realty Income: Attractive For Income Investors, But Mr. Market May Be Trying To Tell Us Something (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Realty Income (O) has been a popular REIT amongst investors for years, especially those seeking income.

With a long track record of paying dividends and likely to continue for the foreseeable future, O deserves their spot in any income-focused investor's portfolio.

Realty Income's A-rated balance sheet, size & scale advantages, and long operating history are something to be commended. However, with the recent underperformance over the past few years, Mr. Market may be trying to tell us something.

One has to wonder if the REIT could continue to underperform some peers going forward as a result of its portfolio, size, and investments that haven't moved the needle as expected.

In this article, I discuss Realty Income's latest quarter, fundamentals, and why their underperformance may be an indication of something investors should be cautious of going forward.

Previous Buy Thesis

I last covered Realty Income in May in an article titled: Why Realty Income Deserves A Spot In Every Dividend Investor's Portfolio. During their Q1, the REIT saw modest AFFO growth of a penny to $1.06 from the previous quarter.

And despite the decline in occupancy, revenue managed to increase due to annual rent escalators. Year-over-year, their bottom line increased 2.9%.

At a price of $55.78, O offered upside to their price target of $61.38. Since, O is up 5% in comparison to the S&P (SP500), up nearly double at 9.84%.

Latest Quarter Saw A Modest AFFO Decline

Realty Income reported their Q2 earnings at the beginning of the month and saw AFFO decline modestly from the previous quarter and year. AFFO amounted to $1.05, down a penny from Q1 and the previous year's quarter.

Core FFO, however, was in-line with analysts' estimates at $1.05, while revenue of $1.41 billion beat by a solid $0.8 million. This increased from $1.34 billion the year prior, likely due to annual rent escalators.

The rent recapture rate was 103.4% across 346 leases, which represented $97 million in annual rent. But this was down from the previous year's second quarter that saw rent recapture at 105.7%. But the number of leases was significantly less at 99.

The REIT continued its focus in Europe, which they entered into in 2019. Management made 57% of its $1.2 billion in investments in Europe and the rest in the U.S.

And this is likely due to them being able to see higher spreads, which investments had a weighted-average cash yield of 7.2%. In the U.S., investment spreads were 7%.

Moreover, spreads also compressed from 2024 as investments had a weighted-average cash yield of 7.9% in Europe during that time. In the U.S., this was 7.6%, 60 basis points higher.

O also disposed of 73 properties in the quarter, most of these vacant for proceeds of $117 million. Occupancy also increased 10 basis points quarter-over-quarter to 98.6%. But on an annualized basis, occupancy declined slightly from 98.8%.

Guidance Raise

Despite the modest decline in AFFO and occupancy, management was still able to raise AFFO and investment guidance during the quarter. Previously, O was targeting $4 billion in investments and AFFO in a range of $4.22 to $4.28.

Now, management expects $5 billion in investments for the year and AFFO in a range of $4.24 - $4.28, representing a growth rate of 1.67% at midpoint.

Management stated in Q4 that guidance assumed a $0.04 impact of disposing of underperforming tenants. This was also significantly lower than 2024, which saw AFFO growth of 4.8%.

Disappointing growth could also be another reason for their underperformance, but this does represent the 15th consecutive year of growth for the REIT.

What Mr. Market May Also Be Pricing In

One of the reasons investors may love Realty Income, besides their long history of paying monthly dividends, is their size, which gives them advantages over peers.

Being one of the few A-rated REITs, this gives them cost advantages, as higher-rated REITs typically see higher spreads over their cost of capital.

As a result, this leads to accretion, which leads to higher growth, thus often positively impacting the share price. Overall, REITs are usually lower growth stocks.

The high-quality, retail REITs often see 3%–6% AFFO annual growth. For comparison, Agree Realty (ADC), who recently raised their guidance as well, now expects growth of 4% for the year.

VICI Properties (VICI), another one of my favorite REITs, is expecting slightly higher AFFO growth of 4.4% for 2025.

Here's Realty Income's investor deck. And you can see when calculating their WACC, they use a retained free cash flow percentage of zero.

Below is the comparison to Agree Realty's investor deck. You can see how management mentions that adding a 0% cost to retained free cash flow improves your cost of capital artificially by roughly 80 basis points.

In other words, this lowers your cost of capital to make spreads appear to be more accretive. While I don't know the correct cost to assign, it seems that a 0% cost distorts spreads and creates the appearance that they are higher than they actually are.

Realty Income's management stated during Q4 '24 earnings that they managed to achieve investment spreads of 243 basis points. Here's what their CEO stated:

Throughout the year, we remained disciplined in our capital allocation strategy, investing $3.9 billion at a 7.4% weighted-average initial cash yield. We funded these investments with attractively-priced capital, resulting in a 243 basis point investment spread, exceeding our historical average of 150 basis points.

You can see from Agree Realty's investor deck that achieving spreads of over 150 bps should result in high accretion, which would benefit shareholders. Moreover, this level of accretion should translate to respectable bottom line growth.

However, Realty Income is only expecting 1.67% growth this year. And while this could have something to do with their portfolio mostly consisting of non-investment grade tenants' underperforming in recent years, possibly achieving lower spreads than appeared is something investors should be cautious of going forward.

While most likely invest for the monthly dividend, which the REIT raised 0.2% in June to $0.269, investors want the potential to see capital appreciation as well.

Moreover, in the future, this may impact their ability to sufficiently cover the monthly dividend. For now, it remains safe with a projected AFFO payout ratio of 75.3%.

Although REITs have lagged due to the high-interest rate environment, ADC is actually in the green in comparison, while O is down over 3% in the past five years.

While the S&P (SP500) has continued to have a strong performance over a 5-year period, the sector has lagged, partly due to the macro environment. But REITs should be viewed primarily as income vehicles, not growth machines.

And looking at total returns over a longer period, you can see Agree Realty has done well against Vanguard's S&P 500 ETF (VOO) in total returns, up 280% compared to nearly 286% for the ETF. O trails behind both significantly, up approximately 106%.

Attractive Valuation

Despite this, O remains attractive, especially if you're a retiree looking for stable income.

So, they have their place inside an income-focused portfolio despite their underperformance. Moreover, they could see tailwinds from expected lower base rates with potential capital appreciation.

Currently, Realty Income's forward P/AFFO multiple sits at 13.77x. For comparison purposes, this is lower than the following peers' average of 14.14x using each REIT's current forward multiple.

Agree Realty: 17.09x

Simon Property Group (SPG): 13.91x

NNN REIT (NNN): 12.08x

Federal Realty Trust (FRT): 13.49x

Wall Street currently rates O a hold with a price target of $62.39, roughly 6% upside.

They do offer a more attractive upside if they can return to their normal multiple of 17.21x. But this will be highly dependent on where interest rates are and if they can achieve accretive spreads and manage to offload non-performing tenants.

Furthermore, I would like to see further dispositions of non-rated tenants to strengthen their portfolio. Management also initiated a share repurchase program that I would like to see them take advantage of before considering upgrading the REIT to a buy again.

Risks & Bottom Line

Realty Income's biggest risk is underperformance going forward. Their large size does give them some advantages, as each tenant only makes up roughly 1% of rent.

But their exposure to troubled tenants like Red Lobster, Walgreens (WBA), Rite-Aid, Dollar Tree (DLTR), Zips Car Wash (who previously filed for bankruptcy back in February), and AtHome, who filed for bankruptcy this past June.

Management stated they expect resolutions going forward and were able to achieve a 94% rent recapture rate during the quarter. But a further weakening economy could result in additional bankruptcies, which could result in rent loss.

Realty Income remains attractive from a valuation standpoint when compared to peers, but their underperformance is something that could continue going forward.

While they could see tailwinds from lower interest rates and subsequent upside, I agree with Wall Street's rating and am downgrading them from a buy to a hold.
